title: Possession
description: Mera, tera, uska — possession words that change based on what's owned, not who owns it.
culturalNote: Possessives in Hindi are more complex than English — they agree in gender and number with the thing owned, not the owner. So a woman says 'mera ghar' (my house) and 'meri car' (my car). Once you get this pattern, it applies to ALL possessives. It's one rule, not many.

exercises.note: 'Mera' for masculine objects: 'mera ghar' (house), 'mera phone' (phone), 'mera dost' (male friend). 'Meri' for feminine: 'meri car', 'meri dost' (female friend), 'meri baat' (my words). The OWNED thing's gender decides which to use, not your gender.

'Tera' (m): 'tera ghar', 'tera phone'. 'Teri' (f): 'teri car', 'teri baat'. This is informal — use with close friends. 'Tera kya scene hai?' = what's your situation/what's up? 'Teri galti hai' = it's your fault.

'Aapka' (m): 'aapka naam kya hai?' = what is your name? 'Aapki' (f): 'aapki baat sahi hai' = you are right. Use with elders, strangers, or anyone you're being respectful to. 'Aapka' is safer when in doubt.

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'This is my phone.' (Phone is masculine.) What do you say?

exercises.note: 'Uska' (m object): 'uska ghar door hai' = his/her house is far. 'Uski' (f object): 'uski car nahi aayi' = his/her car didn't come. Note: 'uska' and 'uski' are the same for he/she — Hindi doesn't distinguish his/her in possessives!

'Hamara' (m): 'hamara ghar' = our house. 'Hamari' (f): 'hamari team' = our team. 'Hamara plan kya hai?' = what's our plan? In Delhi, friends use 'hamara' very inclusively — 'yaar, hamara kya hoga?' = man, what will become of us?

exercises.sentence: _____ baat suno — main sahi bol rahi hoon.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: Listen to me — I'm saying the right thing. (Informal, addressing one friend)
exercises.hint: 'Baat' is feminine → meri (my words/what I'm saying)

exercises.note: For plural owned things: 'mere dost' = my friends, 'teri books' = your books, 'uske kapde' = his/her clothes. Pattern: mera→mere, meri→stays meri for pl too (or mere in some usages). Focus on: mere (my-pl), uske (his/her-pl).

exercises.prompt: Whose car is this? It belongs to Neha. How do you say 'This is Neha's car'? (Car is feminine)

exercises.note: 'Mujhe' = to me (dative). 'Mujhe bhookh hai' = I'm hungry (lit: hunger is to me). 'Mujhe pata hai' = I know (lit: knowledge is to me). 'Mujhe yeh chahiye' = I want/need this. Very different from English — many feelings are expressed as 'happening to me'.

exercises.sentence: Yeh _____ ghar hai. Main yahan rehti hoon.
exercises.sentenceEnglish: This is my house. I live here.

exercises.instruction: Match the possessive to its meaning

exercises.prompt: How do you express 'I'm hungry' in Hindi? (Use the 'mujhe...hai' pattern)
exercises.hint: Hunger 'happens to' you: mujhe + bhookh + hai
